original_abstract: This review explores the fundamental importance, proposed mechanisms, and supporting evidence for five major multicomponent reactions (MCRs): the Biginelli, Hantzsch, Mannich, Passerini, and Ugi reactions. Despite their widespread use in green chemistry and bioactive molecule synthesis, the specific pathways through which these complex transformations occur often remain a subject of debate. We critically evaluate established mechanistic theories alongside contemporary findings from computational chemistry and advanced spectroscopic techniques, such as ESI-MS/MS and NMR. By addressing current controversies and identifying emerging trends, this manuscript aims to consolidate our mechanistic understanding of these reactions to aid in the rational design of more efficient catalysts and sustainable synthetic methodologies.
humanized_abstract: Multicomponent Reactions: Impact, Mechanisms and Trends This review is focused on five of the most important multicomponent reactions, the Biginelli, Hantzsch, Mannich, Passerini and Ugi reactions, summarizing their impact, proposed mechanisms and the related evidence. Despite the large number of examples in which these MCRs are employed in green chemistry and in the synthesis of bioactive molecules, their mechanisms are not always clear and are often the subject of controversy. The previous mechanisms, along with some of the recent computational studies, as well as the new information provided by new techniques such as ESI-MS/MS and NMR are discussed. By summarizing the actual controversies and summarizing the recent trends, this review is intended to contribute to a clearer understanding of these reactions, and in this way to assist in the future development of new catalysts and more sustainable methods.
